title: Persistent Fermi Pockets and Robust Electron Pairing in Lightly Doped CuO$_2$ Planes of Cuprate Superconductors
authors: Hao Chen, Jumin Shi, Yinghao Li, Xiangyu Luo, Yiwen Chen, Chaohui Yin, Yingjie Shu, Jiuxiang Zhang, Taimin Miao, Bo Liang, Wenpei Zhu, Neng Cai, Xiaolin Ren, Chengtian Lin, Shenjin Zhang, Zhimin Wang, Fengfeng Zhang, Feng Yang, Qinjun Peng, Zuyan Xu, Guodong Liu, Hanqing Mao, Xintong Li, Tao Xiang, Lin Zhao, X. J. Zhou
abstract: High temperature superconductivity in cuprate superconductors is generally considered to be generated from doping the Mott insulators. The fundamental nature of the doped parent compounds as well as the microscopic origin of electron pairing remain critical issues in understanding the emergence of superconductivity. Here, using high-resolution spatially-resolved laser angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy, we investigate the intrinsic electronic structures of the CuO$_2$ planes in multilayer cuprates Bi$_2$Sr$_2$Ca$_{n-1}$Cu$_n$O$_{2n+4+δ}$ (n=5$\sim$8). The inner CuO$_2$ planes are well shielded from the disorders and provide a rare and ideal platform to probe the intrinsic electronic phase diagram. We observe well-defined Fermi pockets with hole doping levels as low as 0.007, demonstrating an abrupt transition from the parent Mott insulator to a metallic state upon the introduction of an infinitesimal amount of doping. The innermost CuO$_2$ planes (IP$_0$) display gapless Fermi pockets, while the second innermost planes (IP$_1$) exhibit anisotropic superconducting gaps up to $\sim$33$\,$meV, indicative of robust electron pairing coexisting with strong antiferromagnetic order. Our findings provide a revised framework for understanding the doping-driven transitions and pairing mechanisms in cuprate superconductors.
summary: This paper investigates the electronic structure of the innermost layers of multilayer cuprate superconductors using high-resolution laser-ARPES. The researchers found that even at extremely low doping levels, the CuO2 planes exhibit well-defined metallic Fermi pockets, suggesting an abrupt transition from the Mott insulator. Furthermore, they observed large, anisotropic superconducting gaps in the second innermost planes, indicating that robust electron pairing can coexist with strong antiferromagnetic order. These findings provide a new framework for understanding the doping-driven phase diagram in high-temperature superconductors.
main_problem: Determining whether the transition from a Mott insulator to a metallic state in cuprates is intrinsic or disorder-driven, and understanding the origin of electron pairing in the lightly doped regime.
main_result: Discovery of persistent, gapless Fermi pockets in the innermost CuO2 planes at extremely low doping (p=0.007) and the observation of robust, large anisotropic superconducting gaps (up to 33 meV) in the second innermost planes.
method: High-resolution, spatially-resolved laser angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (laser-ARPES) using an ARToF electron energy analyzer to probe intrinsic electronic structures.
model_or_system: Multilayer Bi-based cuprate superconductors (Bi2223 and higher-order n=5-8 structures) where inner CuO2 planes are shielded from disorder.
key_observables: Fermi surface topology (pockets, arcs), hole doping level (p), superconducting gap magnitude and anisotropy, and spectral weight distribution.
important_parameters: Hole doping levels (p) from 0.007 to 0.088; superconducting gaps up to 33 meV; hopping parameters (t, t', t'').
main_assumptions: The inner CuO2 planes are sufficiently shielded from external impurities to provide a disorder-free environment.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows crystal structures and Fermi surface mappings for different layers and dopings; subsequent figures show EDC peaks, spectral weight asymmetry, and gap evolution.
paper_structure: Introduction to the Mott insulator-to-metal transition; Experimental setup and sample characterization; Results showing Fermi pocket persistence and doping evolution; Discussion of pairing mechanisms and coexistence with AFM order; Conclusion.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Observation of Fermi pockets in multilayer Bi-based cuprates and their elec- tronic characteristics. a, Schematic crystal structures of Bi2245, Bi2256, Bi2267 and Bi2278. The innermost CuO2 plane(s) is labelled as IP0 while the outermost planes are labelled as OP. The second and third inner planes are labelled as IP1 and IP2, respectively. b-g, Fermi surface mappings measured at 15 K. Hole-like IP0 and IP1 Fermi pockets are clearly observed in region 2 of the UD30K-Bi2223 sample (b, 5-layer), region 2 of the UD95K-Bi2223 sample (c, 5-layer), region 4 of the OP111K-Bi2223 sample (d, 5-layer), region 1 of the UD95K-Bi2223 sample (e, 6-layer), region 3 of the OP111K-Bi2223 sample (f,...

FIG. 2. Typical band structures of Fermi pockets and spectral weight distribution along the Fermi pocket measured in region 1 (6-layer) of the UD95K-Bi2223 sample at 15 K. a-d, Band structures measured along different momentum cuts (lower panels) and the corresponding MDCs at the Fermi level (upper panels). The locations of the momentum cuts are indicated by black lines in the inset of d. The observed IP0, IP1 and OP bands are marked by blue, red and green arrows, respectively. The IP0 bands are fitted by parabolic functions (dashed black lines), with the band top located at ∼11 meV. The MDCs are fitted by multiple Lorentzians so each peak area can be obtained. e, MDCs at the Fermi level...

FIG. 3. Momentum-dependent photoemission spectra and energy gaps along the Fermi pockets measured at 15 K. a,b, EDCs measured along the IP0 Fermi pockets in region 2 of the UD30K-Bi2223 sample (a, 5-layer) and region 4 of the OP111K-Bi2223 sample (b, 5-layer). The Fermi momenta are indicated by open circles in the upper panels and represented by the angle θ, as defined in the inset of k. c-e, EDCs measured along the IP1 Fermi pockets in region 2 of the UD30K-Bi2223 sample (c, 5-layer), region 1 of the UD95K-Bi2223 sample (d, 6-layer) and region 2 of the OP111K-Bi2223 sample (e, 6-layer). Fermi momentum positions are marked by open circles and represented by the angle θ. f-j, Symmetrized...

FIG. 4. Formation of the Fermi pockets in terms of the mean-field t-U model and intrinsic doping evolution of the CuO2 plane in the antiferromagnetic Mott insulating state. a,b, Simulated band structures along high-symmetry directions (0,0)–(π,π)–(π,0)–(0,0) (a) and (π,0)–(0,π) (b) based on the mean-field t–U model using t = 0.32 eV, t′ = −0.063 eV, t′′ = 0.031 eV, and ∆MF = 0.68 eV. c-e, Simulated Fermi pockets derived from the band structures in a,b by setting the chemical potential to µ1 = −0.556 eV (c), µ2 = −0.609 eV (d), and µ3 = −0.643 eV (e), as indicated by dashed lines in a,b. These simulated pockets correspond to doping levels of 0.007 (c), 0.051 (d), and 0.088 (e), respectively....
